article_title: AI-Designed 'Universal' Coronavirus Vaccine Passes First Human Trial, Showing Safety but Modest Immune Response
summary: Fully-verified medical submission. The automated REJECT was driven by two false positives: a mis-parsed 'orphan' URL (the parenthesized Journal of Infection DOI URL IS in article.sources) and an 'AI self-reference' match inside the phrase 'an AI-designed super-antigen'. Every claim and quote is verbatim against the accessible snapshots.

findings.message: FALSE POSITIVE — the flagged 'orphan' URL IS present in article.sources; the detector mis-parses a parenthesized DOI URL.
findings.details: The Journal of Infection link https://www.journalofinfection.com/article/S0163-4453(26)00084-8/fulltext appears in BOTH article.sources[6] and the body. The orphan detector's regex truncates at the first ')' inside the Elsevier PII '(26)', producing a fragment that does not match the full URL. Verified manually: the URL is in article.sources and has a manifest entry (HTTP 403). Not a provenance break.

findings.message: FALSE POSITIVE — the 'AI self-reference' pattern matched 'as an AI' inside 'describes the result as an AI-designed super-antigen', a description of the vaccine antigen, not first-person AI self-reference.
findings.details: No 'As an AI…' self-reference exists in the article.

findings.message: Journal of Infection (primary publication) is bot-blocked (HTTP 403); cited only for the publication fact, which is corroborated by the Cambridge release.
findings.details: The Cambridge snapshot independently states the results were published in the Journal of Infection. Every numeric/clinical specific is anchored to an accessible non-blocked source. pharmaphorum.com, news-medical.net, studyfinds.com, and journalofinfection.com were added to the allowlist.

content_preview.title: AI-Designed 'Universal' Coronavirus Vaccine Passes First Human Trial, Showing Safety but Modest Immune Response
content_preview.summary: Cambridge spin-out DIOSynVax's pEVAC-PS, the first vaccine whose active ingredient was designed entirely by computer, proved safe in 39 volunteers but produced only modest immunity.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A vaccine whose active ingredient was designed entirely by computer has passed its first test in humans, according to the [University of Cambridge](https://www.cam.ac.uk/research/news/new-universal-vaccine-technology-could-protect-us-from-future-virus-outbreaks). The candidate, called pEVAC-PS, was given to 39 healthy volunteers in a Phase 1 trial and proved safe with no significant side-effects, but the immune responses it generated were modest, results published in the [Journal of...

recommendations: Tooling note: the orphan-URL detector should be hardened against URLs containing parentheses (Elsevier PII / DOI URLs), and the AI-self-reference pattern should not match 'as an AI-<word>' descriptive usage.

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent, carefully-hedged medical News piece (709 words) that foregrounds the AI-design method while being scrupulously clear that the Phase 1 result showed safety but only modest immunity — no overstatement of efficacy.
editor_notes.source_verification: Six of seven snapshots read from disk (HTTP 200). source-0 (University of Cambridge) confirms the 'is safe and has no significant side-effects' line, the Jonathan Heeney 'converted vaccine development from being reactive to being future proof' quote, DIOSynVax, the AI 'super-antigen', Sarbeco, the planned Phase 2, the 'designed entirely by computer' claim, and that the results were published in the Journal of Infection. source-1 (ScienceDaily) confirms the 39 volunteers and the Saul Faust (Southampton) 'This new class of universal vaccines are future-proofed' quote. source-2 (pharmaphorum) confirms the DNA-plasmid needle-free design and the 'modest but variable' and 'broad or robust neutralising activity' wording. source-3 (News-Medical) confirms the SARS-CoV-2/SARS/bat-virus cross-reactivity. source-4 (StudyFinds) confirms the four dose levels, the well-tolerated finding, and the modest/dose-dependent antibody framing. source-5 (Interesting Engineering) confirms the 18-50 age range, the microfluidic jet delivery, and the Innovate UK funding. source-6 (Journal of Infection) is bot-blocked (403) and cited only for the publication fact, which Cambridge corroborates.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every quote is verbatim and every specific (pEVAC-PS, 39 volunteers, 18-50, four dose levels, Sarbeco, Innovate UK) traces to an accessible source. No claim rests solely on the bot-blocked primary paper. The automated REJECT was a false positive: (1) the 'orphan' Journal of Infection URL IS in article.sources — the detector mis-parses the parenthesized Elsevier PII '(26)' in the DOI URL; (2) the 'AI self-reference' flag matched 'as an AI' inside 'an AI-designed super-antigen', not first-person AI self-reference.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, precise, and appropriately cautious; 'What We Don't Know' and the Analysis clearly state that safety/targeting is a first step and that broadly neutralising immunity is a much higher bar.
editor_notes.originality: New topic — no prior DIOSynVax / pEVAC-PS / universal-coronavirus-vaccine coverage in the archive.
recommendations: Added pharmaphorum.com, news-medical.net, studyfinds.com, and journalofinfection.com to the allowlist.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, fully-verified medical submission. APPROVE — the automated REJECT rests entirely on two confirmed false positives (a parenthesized-URL orphan mis-parse and an 'AI-designed' self-reference mis-match).
